[![Page semi-protected](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/1b/Semi-protection-shackle.svg/20px-Semi-protection-shackle.svg.png)](/wiki/Wikipedia:Protection_policy#semi "This article is semi-protected.")

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Ski goggles](/w/index.php?title=Ski_goggles&redirect=no "Ski goggles"))

Not to be confused with [Google](/wiki/Google "Google").

For 1969 children's picture book, see [Goggles!](/wiki/Goggles! "Goggles!")

Forms of protective eyewear that do not enclose the nose

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Your_Quota_Two_eyes_that_see_Protect_them_2012_002_7217_xg94hq433_crop.tiff/lossless-page1-250px-Your_Quota_Two_eyes_that_see_Protect_them_2012_002_7217_xg94hq433_crop.tiff.png)](/wiki/File:Your_Quota_Two_eyes_that_see_Protect_them_2012_002_7217_xg94hq433_crop.tiff)

Laboratory safety advisory about the importance of protective goggles, 1955

**Goggles**, or **safety glasses**, are forms of [protective eyewear](/wiki/Eye_protection "Eye protection") that usually enclose or protect the area surrounding the eye in order to prevent particulates, water or [chemicals](/wiki/Chemical_substance "Chemical substance") from striking the [eyes](/wiki/Human_eye "Human eye"). They are used in [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry") laboratories and in [woodworking](/wiki/Woodworking "Woodworking"). They are often used in snow sports as well, and in the [sport of swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)"). Goggles are often worn when using [power tools](/wiki/Power_tool "Power tool") such as [drills](/wiki/Drill "Drill") or [chainsaws](/wiki/Chainsaw "Chainsaw") to prevent flying particles from damaging the eyes. Many types of goggles are available as prescription goggles for those with vision problems.

## History

The [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") and [Yupik](/wiki/Yupik_peoples "Yupik peoples") carved [snow goggles](/wiki/Snow_goggles "Snow goggles") from the [antlers](/wiki/Antler "Antler") of [caribou](/wiki/Reindeer "Reindeer"), wood, and shell to help prevent [snow blindness](/wiki/Photokeratitis "Photokeratitis"). The goggles were curved to fit the user's face and had a large groove cut in the back to allow for the nose. A long thin slit was cut through the goggles to allow in a small amount of light, diminishing subsequent [ultraviolet](/wiki/Ultraviolet "Ultraviolet") rays. The goggles were held to the head by a cord made of caribou [sinew](/wiki/Tendon "Tendon").

In the early 20th century, goggles were worn by drivers of uncovered cars to prevent irritation of the eyes by dust or wind.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Likewise in the first ten years after the invention of the airplane in 1903 goggles became a necessity as wind blow became more severe as aircraft speeds increased and as protection against bugstrikes at high altitudes. The first pilot to wear goggles was probably [Charles Manly](/wiki/Charles_M._Manly "Charles M. Manly") in his failed attempt to fly [Samuel Langley's](/wiki/Samuel_Langley "Samuel Langley") aerodrome in 1903.

In 1875, [Matthew Webb](/wiki/Matthew_Webb "Matthew Webb") attempted to use a form of goggles without a seal (which he called "barnacles") on his first attempt at [swimming across the English Channel](/wiki/List_of_successful_English_Channel_swimmers "List of successful English Channel swimmers"), the intent was to shield his eyes from the splashing of the salt water.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Types

The requirements for goggles varies depending on the use. Some examples:

* Cold weather: Most modern cold-weather goggles have two layers of [lens](/wiki/Lens_(optics) "Lens (optics)") to prevent the interior from becoming "foggy". With only a single lens, the interior [water vapor](/wiki/Water_vapor "Water vapor") condenses onto the lens because the lens is colder than the vapor, although [anti-fog](/wiki/Anti-fog "Anti-fog") agents can be used. The reasoning behind the dual layer lens is that the inner lens will be warm while the outer lens will be cold. As long as the temperature of the inner lens is close to that of the interior water vapor, the vapor should not condense. However, if water vapor gets between the layers of the lens, condensation can occur between the lenses and is almost impossible to get rid of; thus, properly constructed and maintained dual layer lenses should be airtight to prevent water vapor from entering between the lenses.
* [Swimming](/wiki/Swimming "Swimming"): Must be watertight to prevent water, such as [salt water](/wiki/Seawater "Seawater") when swimming in the [ocean](/wiki/Ocean "Ocean"), or [chlorinated](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine") water when swimming in a pool, from irritating the eyes or blurring vision. Allow swimmers to see clearly underwater. They will not be usable more than a few feet underwater, because the water pressure will press them tightly against the face. (Below this limit, a [diving mask](/wiki/Diving_mask "Diving mask") must be used, which allows the user to equalize pressure by exhaling air through the nose.) Examples of these include [Swedish goggles](/wiki/Swedish_goggles "Swedish goggles").
* [Power tools](/wiki/Power_tool "Power tool"): Must be made of an unbreakable material that prevents chunks of metal, wood, plastic, concrete, and so on from hitting or piercing the eye, usually [polycarbonate](/wiki/Polycarbonate "Polycarbonate"). Usually has some sort of ventilation to prevent sweat from building up inside the goggles and fogging the surface.
* [Blowtorch goggles](/wiki/Oxy-fuel_welding_and_cutting#Safety "Oxy-fuel welding and cutting"): These protect the eyes from glare and flying sparks and hot metal splashes while using or near a [blowtorch](/wiki/Blowtorch "Blowtorch"). They are not the correct filters for [arc welding](/wiki/Arc_welding "Arc welding").
* [Welding goggles](/wiki/Welding_helmet "Welding helmet"): Includes all goggles for eye protection during welding or cutting. They provide protection against debris, the heat from welding, and, with the proper filters, the optical radiation resulting from the welding, which can otherwise cause [arc eye](/wiki/Arc_eye "Arc eye").
* [Motorcycle](/wiki/Motorcycle "Motorcycle") riding and other open-air activities: Prevents insects, dust, and so on from hitting the eyes.
* [Laboratory](/wiki/Laboratory "Laboratory") and [research](/wiki/Research "Research"): Combines impact resistance with side shields to prevent chemical splashes reaching the eyes. May also include [laser protection](/wiki/Laser_safety "Laser safety") which would be covered by [EN 207](/wiki/EN_207 "EN 207") (Europe) and ANSI Z 136 (United States). Examples of these include [red adaptation goggles](/wiki/Dark_adaptor_goggles "Dark adaptor goggles").
* [Racquetball](/wiki/Racquetball "Racquetball"): Protect the eyes from racquets swinging in an enclosed area and from impact from hard rubber ball.
* [Winter sports](/wiki/Winter_sports "Winter sports"): Protect the eyes from [glare](/wiki/Glare_(vision) "Glare (vision)") and from icy particles flying up from the ground. Double lens anti-fog ski goggles were invented and patented by Robert Earl "Bob" Smith.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Visible Light Transmission (VLT) or S value describes the percentage of light allowed through the lens.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* [Astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") and [meteorology](/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology"): [dark adaptor goggles](/wiki/Dark_adaptor_goggles "Dark adaptor goggles") are used before going outside at night, in order to help the eyes adapt to the dark.
* [Basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"): Several [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") players have worn goggles during play, including [Kareem Abdul-Jabbar](/wiki/Kareem_Abdul-Jabbar "Kareem Abdul-Jabbar"), [James Worthy](/wiki/James_Worthy "James Worthy"), [Horace Grant](/wiki/Horace_Grant "Horace Grant"), [Kurt Rambis](/wiki/Kurt_Rambis "Kurt Rambis") and [Amar'e Stoudemire](/wiki/Amar%27e_Stoudemire "Amar'e Stoudemire"); they prevent a fellow player from scratching or hitting the eyes when trying to grab the basketball. In most circumstances, a player starts wearing protective goggles to prevent *further* injury to the eyes. They are typically fitted with [polycarbonate](/wiki/Polycarbonate "Polycarbonate") lenses to ensure impact resistance.
* [Gaelic handball](/wiki/Gaelic_handball "Gaelic handball") and [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)"): goggles are worn to protect the eye from injury by the ball. The ball used in these sports is smaller than the [eye socket](/wiki/Orbit_(anatomy) "Orbit (anatomy)"), meaning that it can strike the eyeball directly.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* [Aviation](/wiki/Aviation "Aviation"): In open [cockpit](/wiki/Cockpit "Cockpit") aircraft, such as old [biplanes](/wiki/Biplane "Biplane"), [aviators](/wiki/Aircraft_pilot "Aircraft pilot"), such as [Amelia Earhart](/wiki/Amelia_Earhart "Amelia Earhart") and [Charles Kingsford Smith](/wiki/Charles_Kingsford_Smith "Charles Kingsford Smith"), would wear goggles to help protect from the wind and are still in use today. Examples of these include the [AN-6530 goggles](/wiki/AN-6530_goggles "AN-6530 goggles").
* [Virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality"): A virtual reality headset, sometimes called "goggles", is a wrap-around visual interface to display computer output. Commonly the computer display information is presented as a three-dimensional representation of real-world environments.
* [Drunkenness](/wiki/Alcohol_intoxication "Alcohol intoxication"): Goggles designed to simulate the vision altering effects of [psychoactive drugs](/wiki/Psychoactive_drug "Psychoactive drug"), in particular [alcohol](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverage "Alcoholic beverage"). Examples include [Fatal Vision goggles](/wiki/Fatal_Vision_(goggles) "Fatal Vision (goggles)"). There are no studies that have found beneficial effects of use of fatal vision goggles on the likelihood that individuals will later engage in impaired driving.[[10]](#cite_note-MeekerSI-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Jewell1-11)

* [![Dark adaptor goggles are used by observers to preserve their natural night vision.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Dark_adaptor_goggles.jpg/250px-Dark_adaptor_goggles.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dark_adaptor_goggles.jpg "Dark adaptor goggles are used by observers to preserve their natural night vision.")

  [Dark adaptor goggles](/wiki/Dark_adaptor_goggles "Dark adaptor goggles") are used by observers to preserve their natural [night vision](/wiki/Night_vision "Night vision").

## Fashion

Goggles are often worn as a fashion statement in certain subcultures, most often as part of the [cybergoth](/wiki/Cybergoth "Cybergoth") subculture. They are usually worn over the eyes or up on the forehead to secure 'falls': a type of long, often brightly coloured, synthetic hairpiece. Fans of the [steampunk](/wiki/Steampunk "Steampunk") genre or subculture also frequently wear steampunk-styled goggles, particularly when performing in a [live action role-playing game](/wiki/Live_action_role-playing_game "Live action role-playing game").

Goggles are also frequently used by [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") and [manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga") characters as a fashion statement. For example, it is an idiosyncrasy of team leader characters in the *[Digimon](/wiki/Digimon "Digimon")* anime to wear goggles. Other notable characters who wear goggles are the young [Naruto Uzumaki](/wiki/Naruto_Uzumaki "Naruto Uzumaki") and Konohamaru from [Naruto](/wiki/Naruto "Naruto"), Matt from *[Death Note](/wiki/Death_Note "Death Note")* and [Usopp](/wiki/Usopp "Usopp") from *[One Piece](/wiki/One_Piece "One Piece")*.

## Non-human

Goggles are available for [horses](/wiki/Horse "Horse") used in speed sports such as [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In some traditions of horse mounted [bullfighting](/wiki/Bullfighting "Bullfighting"), the horse may wear a protective cloth over its eyes.

Goggles have been used on [military working dogs](/wiki/Dogs_in_warfare "Dogs in warfare"), for protection in harsh conditions, such as sandstorms and rotorwash from helicopters.
